title: The Realization Effect: Risk-Taking after Realized versus Paper Losses

paper_tests.result_description: Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.
Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.
Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.
Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.
Participants who realized their losses decreased investment in round 4, while those with paper losses increased investment.
Non-parametric confirmation: paper loss group invests significantly more than realized loss group in round 4.

description: Imas (2016) investigates the "realization effect" — whether the way prior losses are experienced changes subsequent risk-taking behavior. Participants — university students given an initial endowment — play multiple rounds of a lottery investment game, choosing how much of their round budget to allocate to a positively skewed gamble that pays handsomely if it hits but loses the entire investment otherwise. The key manipulation occurs after participants experience losses in early rounds: in the *paper loss* condition, losses remain unrealized on paper, while in the *realized loss* condition, losses are locked in and participants receive a fresh endowment before continuing. The outcome is the investment level in the final round, which captures how prior loss framing shapes willingness to take on risk. The original study finds a striking divergence: participants carrying paper losses actually increase their risk-taking — consistent with a "gambling to break even" motive — while those whose losses were realized become significantly more cautious, pulling back their investments.

title: What You See Is All There Is

paper_tests.result_description: Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.

Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.
Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.
Subjects in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect (naivete ~0.48), while Control subjects show near-Bayesian updating (~-0.09).
Mann-Whitney confirmation: Selected treatment has significantly higher naivete than Control.

description: Enke (2020) investigates whether people neglect the selection process behind the information they observe — a phenomenon rooted in the "what you see is all there is" heuristic. Participants estimate an unknown numerical state after observing signals drawn from a distribution, first forming a directional guess and then providing a precise belief estimate. The key manipulation varies how additional signals are presented: in the Selected treatment, an information source selectively reveals signals that confirm the participant's initial guess, while in the Control treatment, signals are shown without any such filtering. The outcome is a naivete parameter capturing the degree of belief-updating bias, where higher values indicate greater neglect of the selection process — essentially measuring how much people treat curated information as if it were representative. The original study finds that participants in the Selected treatment exhibit substantial correlation neglect — updating their beliefs as though the filtered signals were unbiased — while Control participants update in a near-Bayesian manner, with the difference between conditions being large and highly significant.

description: Enke and Graeber (2023) investigate how cognitive uncertainty — people's subjective confidence in their own valuations — shapes decisions under risk. Participants on Prolific state their willingness to pay for binary lotteries that pay a prize with some probability and nothing otherwise, using an incentive-compatible BDM mechanism to elicit certainty equivalents. The key manipulation varies the probability of winning across a wide range, from very low to very high, allowing the authors to trace out how valuations deviate from expected value at different probability levels. The outcome is the normalized certainty equivalent — the stated willingness to pay divided by the prize amount — which captures how much weight subjects effectively place on the probability of winning relative to its actuarial value. The original study finds the classic probability weighting pattern: at low probabilities, subjects substantially overvalue lotteries relative to expected value, while at high probabilities they undervalue them — and this distortion is strongest among participants who report the most cognitive uncertainty about their own valuations.

paper_tests.result_description: At high Bayesian posteriors (~85%), stated beliefs are underestimated. One-sample t-test of stated belief against Bayesian posterior 0.85.

description: Enke and Graeber (2023) investigate whether people's probabilistic beliefs are systematically compressed toward fifty-fifty when the correct answer is difficult to compute. Participants observe a sequence of colored balls drawn from one of two bags — each containing a different mix of red and blue balls — and then state how likely it is that the balls came from a particular bag. The key manipulation varies the Bayesian posterior — the objectively correct probability — so that some updating tasks have low correct answers and others have high correct answers, allowing researchers to test whether stated beliefs deviate symmetrically toward the midpoint. The outcome is the stated posterior belief, which captures how well participants approximate rational Bayesian updating and whether cognitive difficulty causes them to hedge toward uncertainty. The original study finds that beliefs are significantly compressed toward 50%: when the correct posterior is low, participants overestimate it, and when the correct posterior is high, they underestimate it — a pattern consistent with people shrinking their reports toward the prior when they are less sure of their reasoning.

paper_tests.result_description: Adding a zero has no effect in Charity/Charity (neutral condition), confirming the effect is driven by motivated reasoning.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether people make systematic errors in evaluating charitable giving — and whether those errors are driven by self-interest. Participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk make a series of binary choices between an outside option and a "bundle" — a sum of several small amounts donated to the Make-A-Wish Foundation. The key manipulation is subtle: some bundles have a zero added as an extra summand, which should not change the bundle's value but may give participants an excuse to reject it. Critically, this manipulation is tested across two conditions — Self/Charity, where the outside option is money for the participant, and Charity/Charity, where both options benefit charity — to distinguish genuine mistakes from motivated reasoning. The outcome is the zero effect — the change in how often participants choose the bundle when a meaningless zero is added — which captures whether people exploit trivial pretexts to act selfishly. The original study finds that adding a zero significantly reduces bundle choice in the Self/Charity condition but has no effect in Charity/Charity, suggesting the errors are not innocent mistakes but are instead motivated by self-interest.

outcome_variables.name: Bundle Choice Rate With Zero Added

Underestimate condition shows less correlation neglect than control, confirming directional asymmetry.

Overestimate condition shows more correlation neglect (larger negative error) than control condition.
Underestimate condition shows less correlation neglect than control, confirming directional asymmetry.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether people's tendency to neglect correlation in information sources is amplified when doing so serves a motivated direction — a phenomenon they call "motivated errors." Participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk predict a number equal to the average of four underlying estimates, given one estimate directly and three news channels that each average that same estimate with one of the others — making the information sources correlated. The key manipulation varies incentives across conditions: in the overestimate condition, participants earn a bonus for guessing above the true answer, in the underestimate condition they earn a bonus for guessing below it, and in the control condition there is no directional bonus. The outcome is mean estimation error — the difference between a participant's guess and the correct average — which captures whether correlation neglect is larger when it aligns with a financially motivated direction. The original study finds a striking asymmetry: participants in the overestimate condition show significantly more correlation neglect than control, producing larger negative errors, while those in the underestimate condition show less neglect than control, confirming that people selectively exploit correlated information when it serves their incentives.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether people make larger errors when they are financially motivated to err in a particular direction — a phenomenon they call "motivated errors." Participants on Amazon Mechanical Turk answer four trivia questions — such as how long light takes to travel from the Sun to Jupiter or the population of Uzbekistan — and before each question, they are shown a random anchor number that is either well above or well below the true answer. In the control condition, participants are simply paid for accuracy, while in the overestimate and underestimate conditions, participants receive additional payment for errors in the incentivized direction, creating a tension between accuracy and bias. The outcome is mean estimation error — the average deviation from the correct answer — which captures whether financial incentives cause people to lean into the anchor's pull rather than resist it. The original study finds a striking asymmetry: participants incentivized to overestimate show significantly more anchoring bias than the control group, yet those incentivized to underestimate do not meaningfully resist the anchor, suggesting that motivated reasoning amplifies existing cognitive biases more easily than it counteracts them.

title: Motivated Errors — Anchoring Study B

paper_tests.result_description: Overestimate condition shows larger anchoring bias than control, even with direction-first design.
Underestimate condition shows less anchoring bias than control.

description: Exley and Kessler (2024) investigate whether anchoring bias in numerical estimation is partly driven by motivated reasoning — that is, whether people anchor more when doing so serves their self-interest. Participants answer trivia questions after seeing an anchor number, first guessing whether the true answer is above or below the anchor before providing their numerical estimate — a design intended to reduce mechanical anchoring. In the control condition, payment depends only on accuracy, while in the overestimate and underestimate conditions, participants also earn a bonus when their errors skew in a particular direction, creating a financial incentive to be biased. The outcome is mean estimation error, which captures how much participants' guesses deviate from the truth in a self-serving direction — revealing whether anchoring reflects genuine cognitive bias or strategic distortion. The original study finds that even with the direction-first prompt and a neutral slider starting point — modifications designed to attenuate anchoring — the motivated-errors pattern persists: participants in the overestimate condition show significantly larger anchoring bias than control, while those in the underestimate condition show less, consistent with people selectively exploiting ambiguity when it benefits them financially.

title: Decisions under Risk Are Decisions under Complexity

paper_tests.description: OLS (LLM-resolved): effect=nan, p=nan | The paper reports Pearson correlations across subjects between lottery and mirror anomaly severity; OLS regression is equivalent (same p-value, R=standardized slope), and is the closest available test to a cross-subject correlation of per-agent composite deviation measures.
paper_tests.result_description: Across subjects, severity of classical anomalies in lotteries is strongly correlated with severity in mirrors, suggesting a common behavioral mechanism rooted in complexity, not risk preferences.

paper_tests.description: paired t-test (LLM-resolved): effect=nan, p=nan | The paper reports median within-subject differences between lottery and mirror valuations across all 12 tasks, which is a within-subjects (paired) comparison; since the simulated data has each agent responding to both lottery and mirror conditions, a paired t-test on the within-subject differences (pairing by agent and task) is the appropriate parametric analogue.
paper_tests.result_description: Median subject makes identical choices in lottery and mirror treatments across all 12 tasks.

description: Oprea (2024) investigates whether classic anomalies in risky decision-making — such as probability weighting and loss aversion — actually stem from risk itself or from the cognitive complexity that accompanies risk. Participants value a series of lotteries and their "deterministic mirrors" using multiple price lists, where each lottery is described as a set of 100 boxes containing dollar amounts with one box opened at random, while the corresponding mirror pays the average of all boxes with certainty — removing all risk but preserving the same computational complexity. The key manipulation is that lotteries involve genuine randomness, whereas mirrors yield a guaranteed payment, so any behavioral anomalies observed in mirrors cannot be explained by attitudes toward risk. The outcome is the deviation of a participant's valuation from expected value, which captures whether subjects exhibit the same systematic biases — overvaluing low-probability prospects and undervaluing high-probability ones — even when no actual gamble is involved. The original study finds that the classic "fourfold pattern" of risk attitudes and loss aversion appear nearly as strongly in deterministic mirrors as in true lotteries, with the fourfold pattern retaining 97% of its strength and loss aversion about 80%, and the median subject making identical choices across lottery and mirror versions of all twelve tasks — suggesting these anomalies are driven by cognitive complexity rather than genuine risk preferences.

outcome_variables.name: Deviation From Expected Value
outcome_variables.scale: MPL switching point ($0-$25 for gains, -$25-$0 for losses, $1 steps, midpoint used)

outcome_variables.name: Implied Loss Aversion
outcome_variables.scale: MPL switching point: sure $0 vs mixed lottery/mirror (0.5;$X,$Y), X increases by $2
